Paul's farewell in Acts 20 confronts us with the reality of Christian sacrifice that modern believers often avoid. This is not a sentimental goodbye but a man staring into the face of suffering, fully aware of what awaits him, yet pressing forward with unshakable conviction. Notice the pattern: Paul travels through Macedonia and Greece, strengthening churches. Jews plot against him. He changes his route. He speaks so long at Troas that a young man falls asleep and tumbles from a window—and Paul raises him from the dead, then returns upstairs to keep teaching until dawn. This is not a man rushing through spiritual obligations. This is someone consumed by a calling that transcends personal comfort and safety. Even the mundane details matter: the lamps burning in the upper room, the young man named Eutychus drowsy in the window, the bread broken together. These are the ordinary textures of a community gathered in Christ, disrupted by genuine danger and divine intervention, yet continuing without hesitation.

Paul's speech to the Ephesian elders is his testimony to what costs nothing less than everything. He reminds them of his ministry marked by "great humility and with tears," his refusal to hold back the hard truths they needed to hear, his proclamation of repentance and faith both publicly and house to house. But the weight of his message comes in verses 22-24: the Holy Spirit is compelling him toward Jerusalem, he knows chains and afflictions await him, and he accepts this because his life means nothing compared to finishing his course and completing the ministry Christ gave him. This is not bravado. It is the settled conviction of a man who has counted the cost and found his life expendable in light of the gospel's worth. Paul does not ask the elders to rejoice with him; he warns them to guard themselves and the flock, because false teachers will arise even from within their ranks. The church, purchased by God's own blood through Christ, is under constant siege.

What makes this chapter particularly piercing is Paul's declaration of innocence: "I am innocent of the blood of all men." He means that he has not withheld from them anything they needed to hear about God's will. The elders' tears as they embrace him on the dock are not just sadness at parting—they know he is walking toward imprisonment and danger. Yet Paul does not console them with false promises. He commits them to God and to the word of His grace, which alone can build them up. He even models generosity by recounting his own labor to support himself and others, reminding them of Jesus's words that it is more blessed to give than to receive. This chapter asks you to examine whether you truly believe your life is expendable for Christ's sake, and whether you are willing to speak hard truths that others need to hear, even when it costs you safety and comfort. The gospel requires not compromise but conviction—and the willingness to let it cost you everything.
